speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This conversation with Clara Payne Grove took place at her home in Moscow on May 11th, 1976. Interviewer Sam Schrager.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: Someone would come and ask where John Smith lives and they would say, Well, right over there on the other side of the creek do you see that cow staked out there?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: That's Mowers cow. He stakes it out there every day and you go past Mowers cow and then you go past the first log house and the second log house that you come to is John Smith. That's the way people were directed in that day by Mowers cow so they called it Moscow. Now, if there's any truth in that I don't know.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: But, anyway, I'm sure you'll be helpful by remembering about a boy. I will.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And the other thing I wanted to know, now do you think that just gently and friendly without any fists we could talk politics a little bit?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: Sure, if you want to. Of course. Well, it's not that I'm so green horn about politics. My father was a politician first and if there was any time left he'd be a father but he was a politician first always. And I grew up on politics and until just the last very few years I have been very active in politics but my mind doesn't carry anything over. I get something and it deserts me. And I would like a little bit of information.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: Now what is it, four elections we have coming on?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: I think it is.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: In the primaries?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: Well, primary or something and I don't know how we have four or what they're about.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: Well, you know, the way I understand what it is.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: He was so known or that people so much wanted him or anything but he was elected on the slogan, the way to reduce taxes is to spend less money. They elected him governor and North Dakota it is said never had so prosperous a time as while he was governor. And he reduced taxes. Well, I think that any man who can grasp the situation in this country to the extent that he can reduce throwing our money away should be in the White House regardless of politics or anything else. But I just haven't known anything about any of these men. And I think that voting is such an obligation that since people are becoming so negligent about it, so refusing to vote, that if nothing else there should be a cash or jail fine for anyone mentally and physically capable of voting and not voting. I think they should be compelled to vote and vote as they please. I don't know how it would come out but in my idea that's a fair way to do it. So I'm glad to hear anything about these men.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: There's one more thing I can say about...

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: ...going to progress now that's certain. And the way that the rural has been and is being administered is death to our country if it keeps on. To me, anybody, anybody, you, me or anybody else would be better than Ford. I wouldn't care who it was. There couldn't be any worse.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why do you feel that way?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: Well, so far as any religious belief is concerned, I think that's all hooey. I think it's assumed for effect. It may not be. It may be better than what I have in that mind. But it doesn't impress me as being sincere.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: I asked for a donation of money.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: And so this A said, are you hungry?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: And B said, yes I'm hungry. He said, well I'll gladly take you to a meal. Says here's a counter, come on in and I'll get you a meal. The man said, no I don't want a meal, I want money.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And A said, well if you're hungry, why do you want money instead of food?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: He said, because I'm a communist, if I can get a dollar, I'll buy one loaf of yesterday's bread and get along with that and give the rest of the money to the communist party. I have seen a man do stunts just like that. So I know something about the communist party. And they work so slowly, so methodically. I'm going to be 97 now in just a couple of days. And way back when I was in my early 20s, a minister of the gospel who was touring and speaking in school houses or wherever he could get a group.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: He said, what is to prevent the Russians coming across that very narrow gulf of water between Russia and the United States and taking over the United States? He said, what's to prevent that?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: Well I'm 97 now and that was when I was in my early 20s that that man saw that. No one else that I ever heard of had heard of such a thing at that time. Well they haven't necessarily come across that straight, they've mostly come by air with our money. And in my opinion, they are getting very close to the place where they'll say take down your flag.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: I'd like to ask you something. You just said a little while ago that you mentioned one religion. You mentioned that communists said that if the Christians had been as strong in their Christianity they could have had one religion and one faith.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And I'm wondering, in the early days, was Christianity more that way within the towns and the communities? Was it more one faith than it is now?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: Well, at that time,

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: I think Christianity depended on church membership as much as it does now. But I do think that the nation as a whole, or at least any part of it in which I was, and I circulated around quite a little, that any part of it lived nearer to the concept as given us in the scripture than they have through the years since. I think that it is that gradually, from the time I was a child until right now, we have gradually drifted away from the scripture to man -made rules only. And that the communists have dominated the world more than any other concept of what they are after.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: When you say living closer to the scriptures than...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Closer? What do you mean by that? I mean, how did that make people live?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: Well, we didn't have rape and murder and burglary as the common diet. There was so little of it that it was almost unknown. We never had prisons that were overfull. In the town where I lived for some years in early life, before I was a teenager and a little while afterward,

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: there were times that there was nobody in our jail. And there were times that there would be one or two or possibly three for a little while. But a jail was just, well, something you had because it was part of the city set up, not that you really needed it or anything of that kind. And as for rape, it was practically unknown. And as for murder, there were so few that you scarcely knew the word. And as for burglary, there was very little of it. Very little. The moral of the people was much higher than it is now. And as for people living together, unmarried, in our town, my father worked on the railroad and in that way I knew some railroad families that I wouldn't have known otherwise. One of them was a family named Cooper, and Mr. Cooper was much older than his wife. And they had two boys and a baby girl. At the time that this Mr. Cooper applied for his Civil War pension, well, of course, he hadn't thought that they were going to look up his record all his life before they'd grant a pension, which of course they do. And they found that at that time he had a wife living in the East. Well, they wrote to him that since he had a wife in the East who was living, and he claimed a wife here, that no pension would be granted. To him, that was absolutely penitentiary. No way around. The judge wouldn't judge anything else and they wouldn't fail to pick him up. And he read his letter and he just simply got out of town. Nobody knew where he went or just exactly when. Well, there was this young wife, the older boy was, I think about 11, something like that. And then a younger boy and the baby girl. Well, there she was, and there was no welfare at that time. There she was without any support at all. Well, I don't know how she got along. I do know that the older boy was working on a ranch and broke his arm and had to be taken home with a broken arm. But to get a doctor to set it probably wouldn't take more than five dollars, all told. And there was no hospital. He would be at home, of course. So that it wasn't like it is now at all. But that was punishable. That was penitentiary. And this man knew that immediately he would be picked up and he would be judged and he would be put in the penitentiary. And they never heard of him again in the town.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: You know, when you talk about his wife, his young wife, it makes me wonder with no welfare what a woman who had been abandoned by her husband or had no husband but children, her husband say even had died.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And what she could, what her situation was, was she in dire straits?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: Apparently not.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: I don't mean just her, but I mean as a kind of in general, when women found themselves in this kind of situation.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: Well, there was county aid. That was entirely a personal, individual matter. I presume that she applied for county aid in which the county commissioners would take her circumstance and judge her circumstance on her circumstance and make their decision as to what county aid she would get. It had no relation to anything former or coming. It was at the moment that someone wanted county aid and the county commissioners ruled on that case as they saw it. And I suppose that she got county aid. I don't know how much. It wouldn't be any great sum, but they owned their home. And of course, she would own it now. And well, I suppose that $35 a month would have been sufficient to keep them. It probably would have been.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What kind of prospects would a woman in that kind of situation, not only her, but when that would happen to people, what kind of prospects would they have?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: If you had children in a world where I take it, it wasn't so easy to make a living, when it was difficult.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would she have to try to remarry or would women be able to go it on their own successfully?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: It is very probable in this case that she would remarry. Now, I do not know about her following this event. For one thing, I was too young at the time to have any object in pursuing it. Well, I'm really thinking of, you know, in general, not just for this particular case, because I'm wondering how society took care of people who were in... Remarriage was very probable for anyone in that situation. Very probable. Because the country was new enough that there was no shortage of men. And there would be, say, a farmer who would be so glad to have a housekeeper that he would gladly marry her with her children in order to have a housekeeper rather than do his own cooking after he worked in the field all day. So that marriage was very probable for any such woman. And this woman was still quite young. Her husband had been much older than she was. But that is the only case of that that I knew of there. And that was because they were railroad people. My father was. I had known the family before this happened. That's how I knew about it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were railroad people more like people that were just working on the railroad?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: Were they more likely to have...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: to move around from place to place?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: No.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: No, they were not railroad men. They roundhoused. It was a division point. And there was a two -stall roundhouse where the engines were cleaned and serviced. And these were settlers in the town. They might be there all their lives working in the roundhouse or on the section, one or the other.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would they be likely to hold a little land outside of town as well, or were they townspeople?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: They were just people, just like the rest of them. I mean, they wouldn't be farming as well or trying to... No. No, they weren't trying to do anything else. My father...well, finally there were seven children, but the seventh one came ten years, eleven years, gap between the one above and the last one. And at this time there were six children, although the oldest one was mostly away on his own affairs at this time. But anyway, there were the five of us there, and father and mother, and we paid the enormous sum of eight dollars a month for a three -bedroom house, a good house, in a good location. And we paid eight dollars a month out of that forty. Well, there was thirty -two dollars for food and clothing and school and anything else that might come up. We got along.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: And at times, each of the older brothers worked for some time in the roundhouse. But father's work was solely in the roundhouse.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were they expected to contribute their wages to the family?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: No, they were not expected to any more than anyone else would be.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That is, you mean to his own family? To, yes, your brothers, I'm thinking before they were married, when they worked out, did they bring back their earnings to help your father and mother out?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: They did or did not, as they chose. But there was law on the books that the father could collect the wages. And if he wanted the wages and did not collect it from the boy, it might be that whoever paid it would have to pay it to the father again. Because he was entitled, until the boy was twenty -one, to the wages. But usually, he did not collect it. And I remember a boy that I played round with while I was teaching saying that his dad gave him his time until he would be twenty -one. Well, that was a gift from the father. He gave over his right to the son's wages. But that was done frequently, that the father would make the son a present of whatever years there were yet when he was twenty -one.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would this be decided on by the father's own attitude or would it be more his financial situation? You know what I'm asking?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: It would be more his own mental idea of whether he or the boy should have it. And each one had his own idea.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: And the law was very clear on the matter, so there was no trouble there. It was quite a different time than it is now.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: I'm fascinated by what it was like then.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: Well, it was like this. Probably you didn't even have a lock on your door. You didn't need it. In the hot weather, with that family on his hands, we slept with the door open all night.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: We didn't need a lock. Nobody was coming in. He had no business to. And there came a time after a while that hard times struck the country. We didn't call it a recession or anything of that sort. It was hard times. And we knew what that meant. There would be a string of men walking the rails and ties, and our house was right by the railroad. And they would come to the kitchen door and ask for something to eat. And some of them asked if they might do some work for something to eat. And some of them, after they had eaten, would ask if they could do any work. And some of them didn't say anything about any work. And Mother fed every one of them that came and asked. And we didn't just hand them out something. She brought them in and set them at the table. Fixed them a meal. And someone said to me, uh -huh, your mother's going to be killed at that someday. You'll find it out too. Well, you know, tell a child that. So I ran and told Mother. Mother said, there is no man as dangerous as a hungry man. And, well, none of them was ever in any way impolite or anything. He was merely grateful and expressed his thankfulness for his food. And some of them were hobos. We knew the difference between a hobo and a businessman when we saw them coming. But there were some of them were hobos. And some of them were simply businessmen in their business clothes, just as they had sat in their offices until there was no way to sit there any longer. And there was nothing to do, nothing to be had, and they would venture forth and see if there was any place that there was something.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: And there was another time of hard times after that.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: And it was a little bit different. I was in Des Moines, Iowa for a while at that time in an apartment there for a few days. And a man came to the door. He was a nicely dressed businessman. And he asked me if he could have something to eat. Well, a woman alone doesn't usually have very much to eat right around. But anyway, I got something and gave it to him.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: And he said, Do you have any coffee?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: I said, No, I don't. I said, I never drink it and I don't have any in the house. He wanted a cup of coffee so badly. But he evidently had been a prosperous businessman and business just was failing all over the country. And there they were. And at one time, oh, it was years later, I was living in a cabin on a farm where other people lived. And the other people were gone. And the man came to the door one day and wanted something to eat. Well, he was sort of strange acting, but I always remembered what Mother said about a hungry man being the dangerous one. So I told him, Yes, I'd get him something to eat. And he sat there shoveling it down with his knife and fork as fast as he could eat. And now and then he'd say, A picture machine. Well, at that time, the picture machine was very new. It wasn't developed at all as it has been later. And I presumed that he had cast his money into a picture venture that had failed. And that had turned his mind a little. But he ate his meal and still muttering to himself about a picture machine, he left. I was glad he left because it was very apparent that his mind wasn't right and I was alone on the whole farm. And later yet in 27 when I came to Moscow, there were hard times.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: Did you realize that?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: I've heard about it. 1927. Well, now, unless they have so much money, a certain amount of money, they can't go to university. They think. At that time, they went and took a chance. I was on the campus one day. Every girl that I saw on the campus either had a hole in the heel of her stocking or she had her stocking pulled down into her shoe to cover the hole. Every girl that I met. That's the way they went then. Well, now, if she can't have two or three formals, she just ain't a -goin', that's all. Which is quite different.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: You know, I would like to ask you a little bit about, more about marriage in those days. And I have, since we've talked about it, I've even read some things about it and I'm very interested in understanding better the differences in opinion now and then that people had. Now, one thing is, I have the idea that there was a lot of, maybe more stress, more stress on marriage then, on getting married than there is nowadays. That as women, girls grew up, they just about had to marry if they were going to have a place in their communities. That's the idea I get.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that true, do you think? Were there many women who were not, who didn't marry in those days?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: There used to be that there would be one shotgun marriage in one place in a long time before there would ever be another. Or perhaps you wouldn't be there long enough that you'd ever hear of another. They were very uncommon. And as far in the university, see my daughter was in 27 -31, the four years there.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: And the dean of women was gone on a trip to the south. And there, the women of school.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: I don't have any trouble at all with my girls in that way. I'm strict with them and I don't have any such trouble. And after being gone a couple of weeks, she came back and one of the women who had been taking her place as dean rushed to her. And she said, oh, Dean French, there are five girls here who are pregnant.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: And Dean French had supposed there were none. But in the whole university, there were these five.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: And later, when I was cooking on the campus, that would be after 31, it would be after 33 to be exact. While I was cooking on the campus, there were four boys in the kitchen with me as common. And one of them was a great favorite with me. A six -footer, a handsome youngster. And jovial and just full of pleasantries. And I liked him so much, I was very fond of him.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: And at the place where he lodged, he had an upstairs room. And the stairway went up, oh, sort of semi -privately to those who were on the second floor. And the woman with whom he lodged got the impression that a girl was coming in by night into this boy's room. So she waited until finally she knew that a girl had been up there for the night. And she went up to the room and she picked up the evidence that the girl had been there. She hadn't quite taken all her clothes away with her. Well, she picked up the garment and took it to the Dean of Men. The Dean of Men called this boy in and explained to him that this was the university and these things were not tolerated. And that if he and the girl both would just quietly withdraw without any furor, without saying anything to anybody, just leave, that they would be allowed to leave in that way. Well, I'm not going to say his real name, I'm going to call him Joe. Joe didn't come for his work one noon.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: And I said to his cousin, who also was in the crew, where is Joe?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: Oh, he's around somewhere. I said, stop it. I want to know where Joe is. Now you tell me where Joe is. He said he's in his room. Well, I went over to his room as soon as my noon work was done up, which wasn't long. And Joe's father, I never can forget how he lived, how he looked. He sat there with his hands hanging between his knees and his head hanging, just the picture of despair. And Joe, the most wretched looking youngster you could ever think of seeing, was busy packing his trunk. And I said to the father, I just had to come over and see Joe. I think a lot of that boy. And his father said that we think a lot of him too. He was taking the boy and the girl back to the father's home, since they had no place else to go. Well, they were married after they got back there. But here was his university career interrupted. He couldn't get a degree.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he ever go back?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: No, he wouldn't be allowed to. No, the condition was that he was gone with the girl. And he never supposed that such an interruption could happen to him, of course. But anyway, that is the way that thing was then. Well, think what it is now, when they're encouraged.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you know of young women when you were growing up who did not marry by choice, who decided they didn't want to be married?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: I know of men who were bachelors, but I'm not familiar around here with any more than one woman who decided she didn't want to get married. Well, I wouldn't know about that. But I would say that contact without marriage was very seldom. Very seldom. I do know in high school. No, it wasn't high school. It was eighth grade. There was a family from far east moved into the town, and they had a boy in the eighth grade. Well, I don't know that being from the east had anything to do with it, but he was from the east. And he taught everything he knew to the boys and girls in the eighth grade. And I went to school one morning as usual, and there was something in the air that you could nearly cut it with a knife. I didn't know what was wrong. And one of the girls ran to me and she said, oh, Clara, they're going to send ID and me to the reform school. Well, one of them was the daughter of the keeper of the upper class hotel in the town. And the other was the daughter of the furniture dealer who was also the undertaker of the town. They had influence enough that they kept their daughters from being sent to reform school, but it was only because they were considered important men in the town that their daughters were not sent to the reform school. I never heard that they were going to send the boys. I suppose they would have, but it was the girls that I heard about. That was high school. Now that was the one case of that that I knew of through school there.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: And later years, it was after I had left there, but I had a sister in that country. And she said that the winter was so terrible that no one would attempt a burial. It was just too terrible weather, too much snow to attempt a burial. But there was a young woman, not a young girl, but a mature young woman who committed suicide because she was pregnant. And that, they couldn't bury her, didn't bury her till spring. That was the only case of suicide that I ever heard of in that place. And it was the only case that came out into the open about pregnancy without marriage. Of course, there may have been more, I don't know, but that was the only one that ever came into the open.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: It was really a very, very terrible sin for that to happen to a person. I mean, for her to commit suicide, she must have been terribly distraught.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: Well, she would be because she was cast out like dirt. She would have no standing whatever in the town. She couldn't face it, so she just killed herself.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: If you got married when something like that happened, would it be alright? Would the town forgive a shotgun wedding?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: Well, I don't know whether she had parents or not. I didn't know much about it except of just these bare facts. But if they had given her a wedding, if she had married, I presume that after years enough, perhaps she would have been accepted. But I think it would have taken a good many years.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: There was one woman I knew who had a laundry. At that time, a washer woman had a different connotation than the words would imply. And this washer woman took in all the night work she could get. And I never knew of her making any attempt to make any friends or to attend anything. She was always at home. As nearly as I know, she was always at home just by herself, not trying to be anybody.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: But while there were those women, they were not to the fore like they are now. Like this woman, they just simply stayed at home and stooped over the washboard. And no times were different then. The moral values received attention in a way that they don't know. And instead of any such thing being looked upon, just with a snicker, it was looked upon, well, like a morass of flames. It wasn't tolerated at all.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: And as far as the church was concerned, quite generally, I think, people attended church. More generally, perhaps, than they mostly do now.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: And the church was made itself more obvious as a place of worship. They might have occasionally some festivity and the church building usually consisted of one room. Some of them had one small anteroom that had been built on later. But a good many churches consisted of just one room. And at Christmas, there would be a Christmas celebration in almost any church. I don't know if it was all of them, but I know that in some of them, they'd have a Christmas celebration, especially for children. And there was the Sunday morning service at 11. And Wednesday, there would be at 7 or 7 .30 or 8, as suited the congregation, a prayer meeting for an hour. And in the evening, at 7, 7 .30 or 8, there would be the evening church service for those who could not attend in the morning. So there were the two formal services every Sunday at the midweek hour of prayer. I remember distinctly our pastor. People were coming later and later and later at a prayer meeting. And he had urged them to be on time. And finally at one prayer meeting, he said, on Wednesday evening, there will be the usual prayer meeting at 8 o 'clock and it will close at 9. And then, well, lo and behold, some of them went in about half an hour later and when the next half hour was up, he pronounced the benediction and walked out. Well, after that, they got there.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: You know, I think that occasions of tact like that are too rare. It seems to me there are so many places that a side effect of tact will do the job. I noticed it so much in the Dear Abby column in the Idahoian that for a while, her only plea was, give him an automation or get a divorce. She came out with that time after time. I don't see that. I don't believe in divorce, for one thing. It is certain that at times there must be a legal separation. Well, in a legal separation, neither party can marry as long as the other one lives. And in a divorce, they can have the judge read the verdict and pronounce the marriage ceremony at the same time if they want to. But anyway,

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: there was, well, I can't remember back there in my childhood days that I ever heard of such a thing as a divorce. Yes, I did. Yes, I did. I was a high school to grade school teacher, the eighth grade. And nosy little kid that I was, I went to the hearing, which made it awfully hard for her. I've thought about it since, how hard I made that for her because she had to witness for her mother. And her mother divorced her father. But, oh, she must have been 35 or so at the time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The teacher?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: The teacher. The daughter must have been at least 35 at the time, or 30 at the very least. But anyway, they were divorced. And I wondered and wondered and wondered about that. And of course, I made up my mind what ought to be. I knew, any kid would. I knew that that woman didn't deserve a divorce. That she'd been all right and she just wanted a divorce, but she shouldn't have it. I remember distinctly that the husband on the stand said that there was one winter that times were so hard that it was almost impossible to get by. And that his daughter wanted a pair of shoes that would cost $15. Well, that was incredible to me. I didn't think that a pair of shoes could cost $15. But he said she wanted them so badly that he got them for her. And it took the last cent he had, and he had to have shoes so he could get out to do the chores in the snow. And he had a cowhide and he made himself a pair of moccasins and wore those all winter. And I thought, well now if a man could do that, he can't be so bad. But anyway, he was granted, she was granted the divorce. And that is the only divorce that I remember of through those years of childhood.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: I must ask you, considering that nowadays divorce is so commonly accepted, you have a strong conviction that divorce is wrong and I think perhaps you might even feel that it shouldn't be permitted. And I'm wondering how, in those, why people felt in the early days that divorce was wrong and shouldn't be permitted.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: Well, they were following the scripture on that. That there was one cause for divorce and no more.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the one cause?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: Unfaithfulness.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: If either one is sexually astray, the other one is entitled to a divorce as though that one had never been married. But other than that, divorce is forbidden.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: But now it seems as though if John says, I wish you'd learned to make coffee, and Mary says, well it's none of your business, I'm going to get a divorce, that's all it takes. They don't seem to have to have any reason. Well, so many of them, as they married, they married with the idea, as soon as the child has a legal name, I'll have a divorce. But if the marriage ceremony contained the information that if you complete this marriage, you can never obtain a divorce, there could be quite a number of them I think would have sense enough not to complete the marriage ceremony and leave themselves free. I argue all the time that pregnancy without marriage is no reason for marriage. That there must be the real reason in addition to that. Or, I say, that neither the father nor mother, nor any parents, nor any person excepting the doctor and the nurse who are oath bound and witnessed that they will never divulge anything about the birth. They should be the only ones who know whether the child was dead or alive, perfect or imperfect, male or female, or any detail of any kind concerning it. And it should immediately go to the parents that have already been arranged to take it. That the adoption should be arranged before the birth. I know a case in which the adoption was several months before the birth. And the ones who took the child for all these months beforehand took this girl into their home and kept her and sent her to the hospital and paid for it and immediately at birth took the baby. That's the way it ought to be done. Although I do see that in many cases the adoptive parents can't take the girl and have her live with them as this couple did. But they were well to do people and they had house sufficient to do so. There was no reason why they couldn't if they wanted to. But they felt that this was their baby, which it was. And they felt that the mother was receiving the care to produce a good baby. And I thought it was just about as sensible a thing as I ever heard of.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: That was, well, perhaps 25 years ago. Quite a while ago, I know. It was south Idaho when I was down there. But we have gone so far with all manner, thou shalt not is what we try to do. Instead of adhering to any of the old tenets as to what is proper.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And did you ever hear of a nation that was taken captive that hadn't gone morally wrong before? What about Rome?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: Men lying in their perfumed baths. Women walking the streets looking for what they were lusting for. Children neglected. So Rome was taken.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: Well, what are we doing?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: The same thing. And yet some people don't see how close this nation is to annihilation. How close it is to being taken over completely. We have, according to all that a person can hear, a large number of senators and representatives who are communist. And the communist terms are being called every so often. I heard Paul Harvey just a few days ago say, he mentioned the sum of 20 ,000 and the sum of 24 ,000. And I don't know which of these two sums he used, but it was one of them. Say even that it was 20 ,000, that the postal department has just spent for a deluxe edition of a book praising the postmaster general. And you pay 13 cents for a stamp, he added. Well, there we are. That's what's doing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that the country was better off without the wealth that we have now? Do you think that being closer to the bone or having less surplus, less money to throw around was better?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: I think that the fact that we have so much money was calculated as a help to losing the country. The fact that we are throwing our money away, the fact that we are giving cash and armaments to our enemies is defeating us. So that those who are back of the defeat say, well, I didn't do it. Your leaders did that. I remember, perhaps you don't, I don't remember how many years ago, it's quite a number. The first money that we gave to a foreign country went to Greece. I think Greece has a president. I think that's their ruler, but anyway, whoever their ruler is refused the money. He said he didn't want it. That was the way it was reported. But it was forced on the country anyway. And later, there was an article in the paper saying that an interview with the ruler of Greece, the ruler of Greece had said, It is true that we have much more money per capita than we had before. He said before, we had $28 per capita, which is a small sum, and now we have a larger sum. But we're in a much worse condition now than we were before we had this gift.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: Well, let's talk about the average person in America. When you were growing up, he didn't have much money. I imagine he had just about enough to get along on.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: We had nearly enough to get along, and we got along anyway. No, there's no use saying we had enough to get along with. We didn't. We had nearly enough, and we got by. And it sort of strengthens the backbone. Have to do it that way. So, better to have not enough than to have too much. It is. It is. When I was a child, if a child had a nickel, it had money. If it had a dime, I'll tell you it was rich. It could get two candies with a dime. At least two. And if it had a nickel, it could get at least one, maybe more, because there were penny candies. And it wasn't so often that a child did have a nickel or a dime. But when they did, they were just up in the clouds. Well, now, they don't seem to think much of a dollar. Dad would give them another one, or maybe a five dollar. And it's ruinous to the morale.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: It's true, there are some children who have to work for every penny they get. And they're allowed to save that money for college or whatever. But that is not the general rule. That's the exception.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: And when I was young,

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: you had to have a lot of preparation for young people. There was no such thing as young people and people and all that. And when they got on in years, they were not the elderly, they were old people. Right out, point blank.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: And there was no for going to church, for going to a social, for going anywhere. There wasn't the smart distinction into age groups that there is now. It's true, there were some things that were for youth and there were some things that were for adults, but not in general like there are now. And nobody ever made an effort to find something that would employ the time of the young people.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: What do you want to take their time for when they have to sweep the floor and wash the windows and iron the clothes, take care of the younger ones and a few more things like that?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: I remember the woman when I was in Melrose -Sager's office. I was there for four very instructive years. And a woman came in one day when Melvin was out and she was talking with me. And she said, if only there was anything in this town for young people to do, but there isn't anything at all for young people to do. Dad can come home so tired he can hardly get his coat off and he can run the lawn mower. Mother can have all kinds of things on her hands, but if the floor gets swept, mother sweeps it. There's just as many things for young people to do as there ever were, only they're not doing them. And it's true that modern conveniences have taken some of the labour and stoop work out of it. But even so, with the modern labour, it's dad and mother that are doing it so that the young people can go to their roost wherever it is and be with the young people. Well, it's alright to be with your compatriots to a certain extent. But I don't care which age group it is, if they separate off by themselves, they get a quirk in their brain that they don't want or shouldn't have. You can take the young people, the children, the adults, the old people, they do not keep the breadth of thought that they would have if they are with the other groups that have other ideas. And it's beneficial to all of them. I don't know just why we got the idea that old people cannot be cared for in the homes of the children or relatives or whatever.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: I don't know where we got that idea, do you?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: Perhaps.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: Perhaps it has to do with this private tendency where people have, each family cuts itself off from the others.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: Cut it up into as many divisions as you can, which is sort of fatal.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: I think some of what you are saying about the age groups, maybe that's a substitute for the way that the community would do things together and would care about not just the family, but it seems from some of what you've said before that it was the whole community that was looking out for the others and doing things together.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that so?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: Well, we did then much more than we do now. We had picnics that took in everybody from the baby in arms to anybody who could get or be taken there. Just the whole community, everybody, they were not divided up, the picnics. They were not divided up for age groups. It is true that in the apartment age that we have now, it would be more impossible for the adult children to keep the old parents with them than it was when people lived in houses. If you lived in a house, even if it was only one room, well in summertime you had some ground that you could all get on. And it was possible, more possible then than it is now, to keep the old people. Unless that is what did it, I don't know what it would be unless it's the restlessness of the age.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What were these picnics like? What were these picnics like in the old days?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: Well, we all took our lunches and went down to the river or out where there were a few trees or to the top of a hill or some appointed place. And we visited around all morning and then we had this noon meal, everybody contributing and eating. And then we visited some more and then we went home. And perhaps they had a game of heart shoes, that was quite popular. And there might be some kind of a ball game or it might be that various children have taken their balls and they just played ball. Our,

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: well occasionally, well about the grade school group would get to playing former in the dale or something of that kind, or a skit to mooloo or something or other. But mostly it was just a matter of being together without any work to do for the day. And we had such good times. And rivers and trees and hilltops were not very common. They were scarce. We were, I think about eight miles from the Jim River and there were trees along that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where was this?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: Redfield, South Dakota. I remember one little girl, that was at Redfield, when I was just a small child. We were all on the playground and this little girl said, I saw a tree that was that big. She put her hands like that.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: Thumbs were almost touching each other.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: And another little girl said, you never did. Trees don't get that big.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: Well it's true, there were trees along the Jim River, but I don't remember that any of them had any size like even these of mine. I don't remember they were that big. There might have been some, I don't know. But anyway, to go to the Jim River.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I'm wondering in those days, if a woman was dissatisfied or wanted a divorce, if she could get one, would people think it's because she had inflated expectations to start with? Would that be the kind of reason?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: Well, I don't believe I would know an answer to that. I don't think of anything on which I would base an answer to it. But at that time,

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: there might have been, I don't know, there might have been just as much differing between husband and wife as there is now. But it didn't culminate in divorce because that was something that wasn't done. In fact, it practically wasn't known about. More than just the word, it was about all the person knew about it. I doubt if, although the concord between husband and wife may have been less or at least different at that time because we were in a calmer atmosphere. At this present time, the atmosphere is rush and push and something different. It's something all of the time. There are no calm places. And at that time, calmness was common. People knew it and practiced it because that's the way they were. This restlessness that we have now, we just didn't know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How would that affect the marriages, do you think?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: Well, if the whole atmosphere was quieter, both husband and wife would be in this quiet atmosphere and they would be more settled and quiet, I think, than they can be now with all this push and rush. I think so much of that is detrimental. Oh,

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: this is...

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: I had the idea about marriages from what I was reading and what we've talked about and other things I've heard that maybe in those days, married people, married couples put their duty before personal happiness.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Does that seem to be a fair statement to make or not?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: Let me repeat it again. Let me get it a little more clear.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: Maybe they put duty, their duty to the marriage before happiness even, that their own personal happiness wasn't as important as their duty. I have that idea sometimes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you know what I mean?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: Well, I think that marriage was looked upon more as a duty than it is now. It was common. It was the style. It was the fashion. It was the time for people to be married and keep their marriage quiet from the notice of other people if there was differing. It was common for people to be married when they were at least pretending that they were glad that they were married when perhaps some of them were concealing something. But it wasn't common for differences between the two to be spread abroad. It wasn't the style.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Does that answer it? I think though that nowadays, young married people would say, why should we suffer if we don't get along with each other? Why should we give up our chance for happiness that we might find with someone who is more compatible?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: That's what I think they would say today, but in those days, that isn't the way people thought.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: That attitude reminds me of the woman.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: It's more than 50 years ago that recent that a group of women were together and discussing a divorce, which was not common. But there had been a divorce and they were discussing it because it was something so strange and so unusual.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: One woman kept the conversation by saying, if I can't get along with the husband I have now, why should I think I could get along with any other one?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: Well, that was quite a bit the attitude. This one maybe isn't very good, but another one might be worse.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that that would be so? That it wouldn't matter very much between one person or another?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: I mean, I have the idea that there are such big differences in people from one person to the next. The people were different. They took hardship in a different way than people refuse it now. If the hardship came, well, hardship, yes, and they just accepted it and lived with it more than people do now, much more.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: And at that time, there was no such thing heard of as a marriage counselor. That makes a difference, I think. I think that perhaps we would be better if we didn't try to put the differences between husband and wife on the same page. On a professional plane. If we kept it so private, just the same as they wouldn't remove their clothes and go into public. They don't open their mind to the public. It isn't done. That was the way it used to be.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know, I would brew a cup of coffee if you would like to have it, would you?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: Okay. I'll put it to brew and let it brew and we can go on. They had a route from the campus in south of here and then around to 6th and I don't know where all they did go, but it was quite a route that would help a good many people. But I guess it just didn't pay out. It was a private bus. Well, people more and more got cars and if they had a car, they weren't going to bother with a bus. But there was quite a bit of travel on the bus when they had it. That was the Neely bus. Neely's had the taxi.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: I'd like to ask you about working and what you thought about it, what your attitude about working was when you were working back in the old days when you were teaching and that sort of thing. I know it wasn't so common.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I mean it wasn't uncommon for women to work, but it wasn't too common either, was it?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: There was nothing like it is now. A woman who had a family never thought of an office job or anything of that sort or in a store or so on. She stayed at home, took care of the family and the home. There was nothing like there is now about women working.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And my opinion is I have to admit that right now they are not saying to a person, What can you do? The employer says, Where did you get your degree?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: And the man must have a degree. And he cannot get it unless his wife puts him through. So I can't help commending that, although I do feel that women working has a great deal to do with the unease of the country. It's not too common that it promotes separation and all that kind of thing. It is not too uncommon that the wife has contact with other men and she isn't making coffee for them, she isn't ironing their shirts, she isn't listening to them tell what a hard day they had, and they look a little glorified when they're not. And sometimes it just works wrong. So it's not a desirable situation.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: But what else are you going to do with it if the man has to have a degree and he can't get it otherwise?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: So I condone that much of it. But as for this woman before, this is ever necessary. And all the years afterward, I don't approve of it at all. Not a bit. I think that it should be motivated against.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: Well, when you were working back in the early days, like when you were teaching and that kind of thing, you did quite a bit of work.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: I taught before I married. I didn't teach any after I was married or work anywhere else. I was at home all the time after I was married. At that time it was very common for a girl to teach until she was married. And the requirements for teaching. I spoke to the Greek letter school teachers one evening down at Johnny's. And one of the girls wanted to know what the requirements were for teaching. Well, if you had graduated from the eighth grade or especially from high school, and you could answer the questions in the examinations that were given you, you were qualified to teach. That was all that was necessary. And just for the questions, they were no more difficult than the eighth grade questions for an eighth grade diploma. I remember distinctly one girl. This was in North Dakota.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: The question was, if a room is twelve feet square and carpeting is one yard wide, how many yards of carpet will it take to cover the floor of this room twelve feet square?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: Well, can't most anybody figure that out. But there was one girl who said to me, well they don't need to give me any question like that. That's just a catch question. And I didn't even try to answer it. She couldn't answer that. She was taking a teacher's examination. Well, I proposed that thing to this bunch of teachers. Ha ha. They were quite puzzled.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: Well, they'd never heard of carpeting a room. And to have that proposed to them, you could see that they were puzzled of what could I mean. It was funny to me.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: Well, let me pursue this working a little further.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you came to Troy and you were working as a newspaper editor, and then when you moved into town and worked here, did you feel that it took a great deal of independence for you to be able to carry on and support yourself and your daughter?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: Well, I just took it as much a matter of course as I would put my shoes on. I had no special feeling about it, only that I was always glad to have work. And after I bought the paper, I enjoyed the work so much. I think I told you how I bought it. Well, I sold it the same way. Just a sudden impulse, a sudden chance. I just accepted both of them. We had planned that I would go on with the paper and we'd get a car and Ethel would drive in any driving weather and live in a hall the rest of the time. But the equipment was all badly worn. In fact, they said that the press was the one that Mark Twain brought across the country with him. And Ethel said, I know better. He never brought any such useless old thing as that. So I don't know whether it was or was not the one he brought. But it was such a huge cumbersome thing that it makes a person wonder if he did bring. Oh, it was practically as big as that alcove. It makes a person wonder if he did bring anything as hard to get across the plains as that would be.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: When you talked about working there at Troy and you explained to me that some of the men didn't approve of a woman editor and didn't particularly like women businessmen. It gave me pause to think that perhaps it wouldn't be so easy for a woman to be a business man in a man's world where men usually run the working situation.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: And I thought, well, maybe it wouldn't be so comfortable at times for a woman. Well, as far as a woman doing it, Troy at that time was quite largely people, a good many of whom themselves were from the old countries. And that is Norwegian and Swede. That was Troy. And in Norway and Sweden, a woman does not bear title to property and own things and do things like they do in other countries.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: But I know the women milked the cows and the women worked in the fields in Sweden. They worked very hard.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: Yes, yes they did. But they had no title to that cow that they milked. They didn't own the field that they tended. The husband owned that, or dad, whichever. And I'm not sure if I'm going to be able to get a title to a car because when I was in Melvin Allsager's office, there was a man came in and he said to Melvin, now it isn't proper that a woman own a title to a car. And my son's widow has his car and she may have it, but I want the title transferred to me because a man should own the title so you get the title transferred to me. Well, Melvin didn't see that.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: So, she kept the car.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you mind working and the work that you needed to do?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: You said you didn't think much about it.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: I had no feeling of inferiority because of what the job was. I was myself just the same as owner of the paper, as I had been sticking the type for somebody else, or as I had been later scrubbing a floor or doing ironing or anything else. No, I never had the slightest feeling that that altered me in any way whatever. I was just as self -respecting at one as I was with another. The only thing I asked was that it was decent, that it was proper. I didn't care what the work was, if there was any pay attached to it so we could eat. We scraped through college in some way. At the very last, I said to my daughter, well, you'd better get a loan of $15, which she did. As soon as I'd recovered from a lot of illness and gone to work again, I repaid it. The woman said, oh dear, I had hoped this would run for years yet so I would have the interest. Well, I wanted the interest myself. I'm going to see about that.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: It smells like it's brewing.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: It smells like it's brewing. It should be. You know, older people lose along with other things, the sense of smell. It's common. It used to be that, well, I could smell a thin mile across the plains. Now, I have very little sense of any fragrance or other than fragrance.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: We were all six of us and five of us married and happily married. He spoke to one sister and said, but you do seem to have in your marriage something that the rest of us missed.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: She said, what is it? How do you do it?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: And she said, I work hard for all the happiness I have. There you have it. Marriage is a business. If you want what it can give you, you have to work for it. And if youngsters were taught from the cradle up that marriage is a business and someday they'll go into business and they must prepare themselves to work at that business. Dear Abby, last night had the question, a girl had to help her mother with the dishes and taking care of the younger ones and she just hated it. That she was going to be an actress or a clothes model and she just hated that kind of thing. And her mother would always tell her that later in life that practice would be useful to her and she didn't think so. And Dear Abby replied, that's what you think. You don't know what becomes of that actress and that clothes model in later life. Well, there you have it. She was going to have her happiness without working for it at all.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: I think it's interesting that you say that it's the children because I have the idea that children are the ones that really suffer in a divorce. Because I have the idea that these days the parents don't really think about the children as much as they used to. I mean they think of the children as little people that can take care of themselves.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: This day, to quite an extent, they don't think it concerns the children. Right across the street here, a woman who lived there at one time across the alley said that every evening when school was out, a family of children from farther down the street stopped in at her place. Well, I couldn't blame her for feeling that she couldn't take all the annoyance of having the two families there at the same time. And yet, my sympathy was with the children because they said, well yes, but when we go home nobody's ever there, so we stay here. Well, you can't blame the children. They went home to an empty house and they were just little tads like the third grade.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: They have no business being alone in a house.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: But you think it has to affect the children a divorce does and it has to just about be for the worse for the kids, the children that are separated from the parents.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: Well, anything unusual that is drastic and happens to a child is very hard on the child. It's just as though you had just the start of a willow tree, just a little thing, about as big as your finger, and you expected it to bear a hundred pound weight, which the grown tree could bear without feeling it at all. It's just the same thing for a little child to have grown up catastrophes thrust on it. And they do suffer. They suffer badly. Some of them tell about it and some of them just nurse it in secret, which is worse yet. So, to protect the children, I would cut off divorce. And if absolutely necessary and really proven, there could be occasionally, like if one parent goes insane, something like that, you know, or if one parent is sent to the penitentiary, there might necessarily be a separation and there might not. It might be a case in which the family should merely wait for the penitentiary sentence to be served, and dad come home.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: How do you feel about divorce when there are no children involved? Divorce what?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: When there are no children involved. Well, I think divorce in any case is just not to be done, except for the scriptural cause. There shouldn't be any other divorce at all.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: And I have an idea that if part of the marriage ceremony states plainly that you cannot receive a divorce, you will be compelled to spend your lives together, that it would settle them down, well, if that's what I have to do, I'll get at it. And I'll stay at it if I have to. It would have that effect.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: But now there's nothing in the marriage ceremony to say that they can't separate. Of course it says about as long as both live, but they don't take it seriously like they would a real statement of the fact that you can't separate.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: No, I'm afraid that if that were written into the marriage ceremony in this day and age, a lot of people would just stop getting married altogether. There you have the point.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: That's what it'd be for, to stop a lot of these marriages. And if you'd stop a lot of these marriages, there couldn't be so many divorces. The person would just simply wait until they had some reason that was proper for marrying. And for that matter, I don't think that love or attraction or anything of that kind is the only thing for a happy marriage. I have seen business marriages that turn out very well. There is no rapture in them, that's true. But they weren't looking for rapture. They were looking for stability. And they got it, and they're happy with it. And they get along just the same as two business partners on the main street get along because it's necessary. I have seen business marriages that turned out well.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you say a business marriage, you mean that when the two partners decided to get married, they did it for business purposes?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: They did it because the woman has no other way to support herself, and because the man has no other way to have the woman companionship that he wants, and to have a housekeeper in his house. Both of them are situated where this is the thing that they can do to achieve what they need. And so I say it's a business. I have seen it. There was a man over home who, well, he was doing the best he could as a bachelor, and he was accumulating property and money all the time, doing very well. But still, when he came in at night, there was his empty cabin, there was the cold cook stove. If he wanted something to eat, he had to produce it. It just didn't suit him. There was the woman who had, she came into the area later, after he'd been there years. And she had a boy, oh I don't know, perhaps eight or so, something like that, whether he was a legal child or not, I never knew, and I doubt if anybody ever inquired. But anyway, they were married, and they had a very good marriage. They married because she had no support, and he had no housekeeper. And there was a splendid young man who worked on the ranch for us, and he was engaged to a girl back in Iowa. This was over in Montana. And his brother and the brother's wife were there at the ranch one day while he was there, and the wife was telling me about Carl's girl that he was engaged to back in Iowa. And she said, we think that Carl had mighty little to do with that, that the girl was the only one that figured that much whether they were engaged. And she wasn't in favor of him marrying her at all. Well, I don't know a thing about the girl except what she said. Carl went back while he was with us for part of the winter. He went back to Iowa to see her. And when he came back, he said to me, Mrs. Grove, I'd like to know how you look at this. She said, my girl has a big job of her own, and she lives at home with her parents who are very wealthy and have a beautiful big home, and she doesn't pay them anything. And on my claim, he had filed land just shortly before, on my claim, I have nothing but a one room shanty. I don't so much as have a horse that I can go eight miles to get my mail from the mail route. And he said, this girl insisted that we be married, and she come back with me.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Said, what do you think?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: I said, well, Carl, the first thing I want you to know is that all that advice is good for is to enable a person to see something on the other side that they hadn't seen yet. But I said, as far as this girl, as situated as she is, coming to what you can offer her now, no, nothing. She shouldn't do it. They couldn't have been happy that way. I don't see how they could. But anyway, it was only, oh I'm sure, not more than three or four months after that, till a new family came into the neighborhood, and they had a daughter who was 16 years old, very grown in mature for her age. But when Carl saw her, he didn't ask Mrs. Groven or anybody else what he should do. He married her right now. Like in a hoop for joy.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: And for anything I know, they got along all right on his claim. The girl never had anything but a family that moved on west and west and west. She was used to the conditions. And I have an idea of she and Carl were pretty well used to each other.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why do you think that rich young woman wanted to marry him and go to the homestead life? Do you think she thought it was romantic or something?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: I have only what I figured out for myself later, and that was that she may have had an illegitimate child, or she may have been unattractive and had never had any other opportunity to marry. And as the sister -in -law said, they felt that Carl had little to do with the engagement that the girl had made it. Whether she had an illegitimate child, that would be very possible under such urgency. If I remember rightly, she was a little older than Carl. This 16 -year -old girl was a few years younger. Or it may be that she just was so unattractive that men were not interested in her at all. That happens at times. And what it was, I don't know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But was it the fact that she was well to do that in itself made you decide that it wouldn't be a good match at that time?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: That she could live so luxuriously and all her life had, and that she had all her own wage and her living free, and then go here where a dollar was a lot of money, and where he lived on the smallest amount of grocery that he could, and that kind of thing. It would be deathly to her after living in luxury all her life. If it had been a love match, but as nearly as I could make out, there was no love whatever between them. It was merely her insistence that they marry and Carl's determination that it wouldn't be best. And I was sure he was right about it. No, I don't think that except with a monumental love for each other, she could have endured that. There are cases in which a woman can take on that and live with it and be gloriously happy. But they're so rare. They just happen once in a lifetime.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: Carl was a mighty good boy. We had another very good boy who married the school teacher way off quite a distance from us where he'd been most nearly.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: I met her, and I think they had two children at the time I met her. But anyway, later I heard that she had gotten to looking so seedy with never getting anything new or not keeping herself up that Matt gave her $100. And he said, now you go to town and fix yourself up with new clothes. Well, she went to town all right enough. No question, she went to town. She came home with 100 turkey pokes for about $100.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: Incredible as it seems. I heard he had a Christmas card from Matt two years ago. He was in a hospital at the time. I don't know anything more about him than that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was she more practical than he was then? Is that the moral of that story?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: I don't think it was practical altogether. I think it was just not laying the proposition out and selecting what she should out of it. Now if she wanted to get 10 or 20 pokes, all right. But she had no right to spend that $100 on turkey. And I can imagine how Matt would feel. He was such a splendid young man. And I heard that he got to drinking very badly. Well, I suppose he was trying to hide his unhappiness as though liquor would do it.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: I was going to ask you that too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that is often the reason why a man would start to drink? To hide his unhappiness or to escape from his frustrations or disappointments in life?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: I think that his unhappiness and disappointment have befuddled his mind to a little extent. That it doesn't function as it should to lay the proposition out and work the whole thing out mathematically as to whether he does or doesn't do this, that, and the other. But I think he isn't quite as capable of doing that. But I do think that unhappiness, not only from marriage but business or ill health or various things, they do start drinking thinking it will stop that. And I think that aside from that, quite a bit of it is that the bunch is drinking and he doesn't have great enough lemonade for mine. He doesn't have the grit to do it. I read of a congressional party, a congressman having a party, and one of them said to another one, he said, as you see I just have orange juice here. He said some people think it's strange but I never take anything more than a fruit juice. And the other one said, well, that's what I have if you notice. He said I never drink anything else. So it can be done. These were two men in the United States Congress. It can be done even if the rest of the bunch has something else. But there aren't so many who have the grit to do it. Just the same as, well, on St. Patrick's Day. Some people put on a green tie and others wouldn't think of being that conspicuous. And they have another grit of not getting in with the bunch if they don't do as the bunch does. We're never thinking that maybe some of the bunch would like to do something else too. There are quite a number of reasons. And at the present time, there are lots of children who are not more than 10 years old who are confirmed alcoholics. I got that fact from a factual source just recently.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Isn't that something awful?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: A 10 year old, a chronic alcoholic. Well, I suppose that a child starts drinking because dad and mother drink. Or that he doesn't know any better. He has no knowledge of what the consequences can be. So if other people are drinking it, well, so to speak.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: The amount that is consumed is too pengous.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: The money spent on it is enough to run the nation in terror. That is, without throwing it to nations that don't want it or are using it to fight us or something of that kind.
